title: Why Does Your Phone Freeze? How Computers Think About Efficiency
description: Why does your phone freeze? Students explore how computers think about efficiency and why a working algorithm is not always a good one.

roleModelOfDay.name: Grace Hopper
roleModelOfDay.intro: A mathematician, computer scientist, and US Navy Rear Admiral who invented the first compiler — a program that translates human-readable instructions into machine code. She famously said: 'The most dangerous phrase in the language is we've always done it this way.'

vocabulary.def: A set of step-by-step instructions that solves a problem
How well an algorithm uses time and resources — a faster solution is more efficient
A compromise where improving one thing makes another thing worse
What happens when you increase the input size — from 10 items to 10 million

learningObjectives: Explain the difference between a working algorithm and an efficient algorithm.
Describe how scale changes whether an algorithm is practical.
Identify a greedy strategy failure and explain why local optimum can fail globally.
Connect efficiency decisions in software to real-world social and economic consequences.
fullDescription: Age group: 12-17. Duration: 30 minutes. No prior knowledge assumed. SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Learning goal: students can explain the difference between a working algorithm and an efficient one, and connect this to technology decisions that affect daily life.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson: Algorithm, Efficiency, Trade-off, Scale. Prepare five sticky notes with numbers 47, 8, 93, 21, 64. Draw the delivery map on the board: A-B 12, A-C 8, B-C 5, B-D 9, C-D 7, D-E 11. Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
steps: Phase 1 (0-2 min): Put spark question on board: 'Your phone has billions of calculations happening every second. So why does it still freeze, lag, and run out of battery too fast?' Collect pair ideas, acknowledge hardware explanations, then reframe: software instructions matter as much as hardware.
Phase 1 hook: Use Amazon fulfilment example. At massive scale, a 1% algorithm improvement can save hundreds of millions of euros and fuel cost, while a 1% degradation cascades into delays.

Phase 2 (2-12 min), Round 1 Bubble Sort: Five volunteers stand as 47, 8, 93, 21, 64. Adjacent comparisons and swaps only. Count swaps aloud. Record scale intuition: 5 items ~10 swaps, 1,000 items ~500,000 swaps, 1,000,000 items ~500,000,000,000 swaps.

Phase 2 (2-12 min), Round 2 Merge Sort: Reset order, split into groups of two and three, sort small groups, then merge by comparing front values. Record scale intuition: 1,000 items ~10,000 comparisons, 1,000,000 items ~20,000,000 comparisons.
Phase 2 visual: Draw two curves on board, steep Bubble Sort and flatter Merge Sort. Label x-axis list size and y-axis work required. Emphasize: same output, very different cost.
Phase 3 (12-24 min): Delivery drone challenge on board map with travel times. Groups write deterministic rules for visiting every warehouse once and returning to A.
Testing (4 min): Execute one group's rules exactly as written. Typical nearest-unvisited strategy yields A to C to B to D to E and fails because no direct route from E back to A.
Debrief: Name greedy algorithm only after students diagnose failure. Add look-ahead rule.
Phase 4 (24-29 min): Choose discussion path based on class momentum.
Exit reflection (29-30 min): Students complete sentence: 'The difference between a working algorithm and a good algorithm is _______.'
discussionPrompts: Your algorithm made the best local decision at each step and still produced a bad global result. Where else does that happen outside computing?
Traffic, climate, and social media all show local versus global tension. Who is responsible when no single actor made an obviously wrong individual choice?
If an app uses extra battery to maximize engagement, is that an efficiency failure or success, and whose objective defines the answer?
If the algorithm is doing its job perfectly but the job itself is harmful, who should change the objective: company, platform, regulator, or user?
If hard optimization problems became easy (P vs NP context), what gains and vulnerabilities would appear first?
tips: Students do not need formal n^2 versus n log n mathematics; operation counts and curve shape are enough.
Push algorithm specificity: if two options tie, rules must say what to do. Computers cannot infer intent.
Do not rescue groups during route design; let the failure reveal itself in testing.
If a group avoids the trap by chance, test whether their rules still work after changing map weights.
Watch for three signals: independent diagnosis of greedy failure, genuine disagreement in discussion, and unprompted links to real systemic issues.

teacherGuide: Core message: correctness is necessary but not sufficient. A computer doing the wrong thing very fast is worse than a computer doing the right thing slightly slower. Move students from 'does it work?' to 'does it still work well at scale, under constraints, and for the right objective?'

studentExercises.title: Two Ways to Sort
studentExercises.description: Compare Bubble Sort and Merge Sort with operation counts, then interpret why both produce correct output but only one remains practical at scale.
instructions: Sort 47, 8, 93, 21, 64 using adjacent-swap-only method and count swaps.
Estimate operation growth for 1,000 and 1,000,000 items under this approach.
Repeat sorting via split-sort-merge approach and count comparisons.
Draw both growth curves and label x as list size and y as work required.
Write one sentence explaining why same result can still mean very different cost.
successCriteria: Students can state that both algorithms are correct but differ in scaling behavior.
Students interpret growth trend direction correctly from curve sketch.
Students connect algorithm choice to practical system constraints.

studentExercises.title: Delivery Drone Route Rules
studentExercises.description: Write and test deterministic route rules, then diagnose why nearest-next-step strategy can fail globally.
instructions: Write exact routing rules for visiting each warehouse once and returning to A.
Test one group's algorithm by literal execution with no interpretation.
Identify where nearest-unvisited heuristic fails on return feasibility.
Propose one additional look-ahead rule to avoid trap states.
State one context where greedy approaches are still acceptable trade-offs.
successCriteria: Group explains local versus global objective mismatch.
Group proposes at least one feasible mitigation rule.

studentExercises.title: Exit Reflection — Working vs Good
studentExercises.description: Write and defend one sentence distinguishing algorithm correctness from algorithm quality.
instructions: Complete: 'The difference between a working algorithm and a good algorithm is _______.'
Include at least one concept: scale, consequences, trade-off, or future-state checking.
Share with partner and revise once for precision.
Volunteer strongest class phrasing for board capture.
successCriteria: Sentence distinguishes correctness from efficiency or consequences.
Student uses evidence from class activities.
Student wording is specific, not generic.

studentChallenges.title: Greedy Trap Debug
studentChallenges.description: Find the exact failure point in a greedy route and redesign rules with minimal added complexity.
instructions: Replay nearest-step algorithm and mark first irreversible mistake.
Write one extra condition to prevent dead-end completion states.
Test revised rules on original map.
Discuss whether revised rule would generalize to new maps.
Document one trade-off introduced by extra checking.
successCriteria: Group identifies specific failure point, not just final failure outcome.
Revision improves feasibility without excessive rule bloat.

studentChallenges.title: P vs NP Prompt
studentChallenges.description: Explore implications if hard search problems became easy, including optimization and security consequences.
instructions: Read prompt about route explosion from 5 to 20 warehouses.
State one system that benefits if hard problems become easy.
State one system that becomes vulnerable if hard problems become easy.
Explain why this matters even without formal proof knowledge.
successCriteria: Student identifies at least one optimization gain and one security risk.
Reasoning connects hardness to practical system behavior.
Response reflects uncertainty responsibly rather than overclaiming.

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these four words on the board — leave them visible all lesson:
Algorithm: a set of step-by-step instructions that solves a problem
Efficiency: how well an algorithm uses time and resources
Trade-off: a compromise where improving one thing makes another worse
Scale: what happens when you increase the input size — from 10 to 10 million
Prepare five sticky notes with numbers: 47, 8, 93, 21, 64.
Draw the delivery map on the board with travel times:
A—B: 12 min, A—C: 8 min, B—C: 5 min, B—D: 9 min, C—D: 7 min, D—E: 11 min
Draw five circles for warehouses A through E and label each route with its time.
Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
Identify five students you can use as sorting volunteers in Phase 2.

lessonSlides.title: Grace Hopper
lessonSlides.roleModel: Grace Hopper (1906–1992) was a mathematician, computer scientist, and US Navy Rear Admiral. She was one of the first programmers of the Harvard Mark I computer in 1944, and she invented the first compiler — a program that translates human-readable instructions into machine code. Before her compiler, every programmer had to write instructions in raw machine language — sequences of numbers that were nearly impossible to read, debug, or maintain. Grace saw that this was an efficiency problem: not just for computers, but for humans. Her compiler let people write in something closer to English and have the computer translate it. She was told it would never work — that computers could only do arithmetic, not understand language. She built it anyway and proved them wrong. She is also credited with popularising the term 'debugging' after a moth was found inside the Harvard Mark II computer causing a malfunction. Her most famous quote captures the spirit of this lesson: 'The most dangerous phrase in the language is: we've always done it this way.' She spent her career challenging inefficiency — in code, in processes, and in organisations.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Introduce Grace simply: 'Today's role model changed how every programmer in the world works. Her name is Grace Hopper. She was a mathematician, a Navy officer, and one of the first people to program a computer. She invented the compiler — a tool that lets humans write instructions in words instead of numbers. Before her, programming was impossibly slow and error-prone. She made it efficient. Her famous quote is: The most dangerous phrase in the language is we've always done it this way. Today, you are going to learn why she was right.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Grace Hopper's compiler was rejected by experts who said computers could only do arithmetic. She built it anyway, and it became the foundation of every programming language used today — Java, Python, JavaScript, all of them ultimately descend from her insight that humans should write for humans and let the computer translate. When students compare Bubble Sort to Merge Sort, they are experiencing exactly the kind of efficiency leap Grace fought for.

lessonSlides.title: What We're Doing Today
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Welcome the class: 'Today we are going to answer a question you have probably asked yourself: why does your phone freeze? It has billions of calculations per second — more power than the computers that landed on the Moon. So why does it still lag, stutter, and run out of battery? The answer is not just hardware. It is software — specifically, how efficiently the instructions are written. Today, you are going to see the difference between a working algorithm and a good one.'
whatToDo: Point to the vocabulary on the board: 'These four words are what we're working with today.'
Point to the sticky notes: 'Those numbers are going to help us see the difference between two sorting methods — one fast, one brutally slow.'
Point to the map: 'And that map is going to show you why the obvious best choice at each step can lead to total failure.'
This lesson is 30 minutes. Keep transitions sharp.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Put the question on the board or read it aloud: 'Your phone has billions of calculations happening every second. So why does it still freeze, lag, and run out of battery too fast?'
whatToDo: Give students 20 seconds to think. Take 2–3 answers.
Students will say: too many apps, old phone, bad wifi, small memory.

Acknowledge the hardware answers: 'Those are real factors. But here is what most people miss: the software matters just as much. If the instructions your phone runs are inefficient — if they do more work than necessary — the phone slows down even with powerful hardware.'

Hook with scale: 'Amazon processes hundreds of millions of orders per day. A 1% improvement in their routing algorithm saves hundreds of millions of euros and thousands of tonnes of fuel. A 1% degradation cascades into delays for millions of people. At scale, efficiency is not a nice-to-have — it is survival.'
Close: 'Today you are going to see exactly what that means — by comparing two ways of doing the same thing.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If the class is quiet, try: 'Have you ever waited for a search result that took five seconds instead of one? That was probably an efficiency problem — the algorithm did more work than it needed to. Now imagine that delay multiplied by a billion users.'

lessonSlides.title: Bubble Sort — The Slow Way
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'I need five volunteers. You are going to become a sorting algorithm.'
whatToDo: Five students stand at the front. Give each one a sticky note: 47, 8, 93, 21, 64.
Say: 'Your job is to get into order — smallest on the left, largest on the right. But you can only compare with the person next to you, and you can only swap with them. No jumping. No skipping.'
Start from the left. Compare the first pair: 47 and 8. 8 is smaller — swap. Count: 'Swap 1.'
Continue comparing adjacent pairs. After one full pass, the largest number (93) is at the end.
Keep going until fully sorted. Count every swap aloud.

After sorting, say: 'That took about 10 swaps for 5 numbers. Now imagine 1,000 numbers. Bubble Sort would need about 500,000 swaps. A million numbers? About 500 billion swaps. That is why your phone freezes.'
Write on the board: Bubble Sort — 5 items: ~10 swaps. 1,000 items: ~500,000. 1,000,000 items: ~500,000,000,000.
Say: 'This algorithm works. It gives the correct answer. But is it good?'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students think 10 swaps is fine, escalate: 'Your music app has 10,000 songs. If it sorted them with Bubble Sort every time you opened it, it would take 100 million operations. Your phone would freeze for seconds. That is the difference between working and efficient.'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Grace Hopper made the same observation: the obvious approach is rarely the most efficient. Her compiler chose better paths automatically so programmers did not have to think about it.

lessonSlides.title: Merge Sort — The Fast Way
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Same numbers. Same goal. Different method. Let's see what happens.'
whatToDo: Reset to original order: 47, 8, 93, 21, 64.
Split into two groups: (47, 8) and (93, 21, 64).
Sort each small group internally — that takes very few swaps.
Now merge: compare the front of each sorted group. The smaller one goes first. Repeat.
Count comparisons during the merge. It will be significantly fewer than Bubble Sort.
Write on the board: Merge Sort — 1,000 items: ~10,000 comparisons. 1,000,000 items: ~20,000,000 comparisons.
Draw two curves on the board: Bubble Sort curves steeply upward. Merge Sort rises much more gently.
Label x-axis: 'list size.' Label y-axis: 'work required.'
Say: 'Same input. Same output. Radically different cost. Bubble Sort grows explosively. Merge Sort grows gently. At a million items, Merge Sort is 25,000 times faster.'

Close: 'Grace Hopper saw this exact problem. Programmers were writing code the slow way because that was familiar. She invented a tool — the compiler — that did the same job in a fraction of the effort. Same principle: working is not the same as good.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students ask why anyone would use Bubble Sort, say: 'Good question. For 5 items, it does not matter. For 50, it is a bit slow. For 5 million, it is impossible. The algorithm did not change — the scale did. That is why efficiency matters.'

lessonSlides.title: Two Ways to Sort
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Compare what you just saw: same result, different cost. Write one sentence explaining why both algorithms are correct but only one is practical at scale.'
whatToDo: This exercise spans Phase 2 Rounds 1 and 2.
After both sorts, students write their summary sentence.

lessonSlides.title: Delivery Drone Challenge — Setup
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Now you are going to design an algorithm for a real problem. A delivery drone has to visit five warehouses — A, B, C, D, and E — and return to A. The map is on the board. Each route has a travel time. Your job: write rules that visit every warehouse exactly once and get back to A.'
whatToDo: Point to the map on the board: A—B: 12, A—C: 8, B—C: 5, B—D: 9, C—D: 7, D—E: 11.
Form groups of three.
Say: 'Write deterministic rules — rules so precise that a robot with no common sense could follow them. If two options tie, your rules must say what to do. No guessing.'
Say: 'You have 8 minutes to write your rules. Then we will test one group's algorithm — literally executing it step by step.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups are stuck on where to start, say: 'Most people start with a simple rule: go to the nearest warehouse you have not visited yet. Try that. See what happens. But write it precisely — every step.'

lessonSlides.title: Test the Algorithm
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Time is up. Let's test one group's algorithm — exactly as written. I am the robot. I follow the rules literally. No interpreting, no helping.'
whatToDo: Pick one group. Have them read their rules aloud, step by step.
Execute literally on the board map. Trace the path.
Most groups will use a nearest-unvisited strategy: A → C (8 min) → B (5 min) → D (9 min) → E (11 min).
Now: 'The algorithm says go to nearest unvisited warehouse. All warehouses visited. Return to A.'
Problem: there is no direct route from E back to A on the map.
Let the failure land. Do NOT rescue. Say: 'The algorithm worked at every step. Every decision was locally optimal — it chose the shortest route available. But the final outcome failed. Why?'
Students: 'It did not check whether it could get back.'
Say: 'Exactly. This is called a greedy algorithm — it makes the best choice right now without checking the future. And greedy algorithms can fail when local optimum does not equal global optimum.'

Say: 'Grace Hopper warned about this: doing what we've always done — taking the obvious next step — can lead to failure. The fix? Look ahead. Before committing to a step, check: can I still complete the full route from here?'

lessonSlides.title: Delivery Drone Route Rules
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Write exact routing rules. Test them. Find the failure. Propose a fix.'
rules: Visit every warehouse exactly once and return to A.
Rules must be deterministic — no guessing.
If two options tie, rules must specify what to do.
Test by literal execution — no interpretation allowed.
whatToDo: This exercise spans Phase 3 setup and testing.
See Phase 3 slides for full facilitation.

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question below that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than two rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
discussionQuestions: 'Your algorithm made the best choice at every step and still failed overall. Where else does that happen outside computing?' Follow-up: 'Every driver takes the fastest route for themselves. The result? Everyone takes the same road and it jams. Local optimum, global failure. Same pattern.'
'If an app uses extra battery to maximize your engagement — keeping you scrolling — is that efficient or inefficient? Efficient for whom?'
'If an algorithm does its job perfectly but the job itself is harmful — recommending extreme content, maximising screen time — who should change the objective? The company? The regulator? The user?'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If discussion is slow, try: 'Think about social media algorithms. They are extremely efficient at one thing: keeping you on the platform. But is that the right thing to be efficient at? What if efficiency at engagement means inefficiency at wellbeing?'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Hopper's most quoted line applies directly: 'The most dangerous phrase in the language is: we've always done it this way.' Every slow algorithm in production is somebody's default that nobody questioned.

lessonSlides.title: Exit Reflection — Working vs Good
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Final task. Complete this sentence: The difference between a working algorithm and a good algorithm is _______.'

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask every student to write and complete the sentence on paper or sticky note.
They must include at least one concept: scale, consequences, trade-off, or future-state checking.
Share with a partner and revise for precision.
Capture the strongest class sentence on the board.
Read 2–3 strong responses aloud.
lessonSlides.exitReflection: The difference between a working algorithm and a good algorithm is _______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Close with: 'Grace Hopper proved that the way you write instructions matters as much as what those instructions do. You just saw two sorting algorithms with the same output but radically different cost. You designed a routing algorithm that was perfect at every step and failed at the end. Working is not the same as good. Next lesson, you will learn how computers make security decisions — and why getting true and false right can protect or endanger a hospital.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If reflections are shallow, push: 'Do not just say a good algorithm is faster. Be specific. Faster at what scale? What happens if it is not? What trade-off does efficiency require? Use the sorting or the drone example.'
